ORIGINAL PAPER
Integrating histopathology and genomic data: a comparative study of
fusion methods for breast cancer survival prediction
Younes Akbari1
· Faseela Abdullakutty1 · Somaya Al Maadeed1 · Ahmed Bouridane2,3 · Rifat Hamoudi3,4,5
Received: 7 March 2025 / Accepted: 1 October 2025 / Published online: 2 December 2025
© The Author(s) 2025
Abstract
Accurate breast cancer survival prediction using multi-modal data is vital for enhancing clinical decisions. This study evaluates
deep learning based fusion strategies, early, intermediate, late, and a hybrid approach, to integrate histopathology images and
genomic data for one year survival prediction. We developed a robust evaluation framework, employing tailored deep learning
architectures and metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC. Model performance was validated using
Kaplan–Meier curves and log-rank tests, with SHAP-based feature importance analysis enhancing interpretability. Results
highlight the strengths and limitations of each fusion strategy, offering insights into optimal multi-modal learning approaches
for breast cancer prognosis. Our ﬁndings underscore the importance of selecting task speciﬁc fusion methods, providing a
reproducible, interpretable framework to advance survival prediction. All code and conﬁgurations are publicly available.
Keywords Histopathology image · Genomic data · Fusion methods · Breast cancer survival prediction

Introduction
Breastcancerisoneofthemostprevalentandlife-threatening
diseases worldwide, and accurate survival prediction plays a
pivotal role in guiding treatment strategies and improving
patient outcomes [23, 25]. The prediction of patient survival
is usually understood as a task of survival time regres-
sion modeling [12]. The Cox proportional hazards (CoxPH)
model is a widely used approach to survival prediction in
which a linear combination of variables determines the log-
partial risk for a patient [39]. In addition, several extensions
of the CoxPH model, including LASSO regularization [39]
and deep neural networks trained with Cox partial likeli-
hood loss [12, 27], have achieved remarkable success and
promising results. Almost all Cox regression studies employ
only genomic data for survival prediction. Molecular data can
provide important insights into cancer patient survival; how-
ever, integrating additional cancer-related information could
improve predictive accuracy. In addition to genomic data,
pathologic images reveal cellular morphologic features that
are closely associated with patient survival [1, 3]. The advent
of digital whole-slide imaging has enabled computer-assisted
techniques for analyzing pathological images, which greatly
improve efﬁciency, accuracy, and consistency [2, 11]. There-

fore, various image-based approaches [10, 35] have been
developed for survival prediction. These methods extract fea-
tures from only pathological images, such as size, shape,
distribution and texture of cell nuclei, which have shown a
strong correlation with survival outcome and pave the way
for further research opportunities. In general, these images
can be categorized into high and low risk, which we con-
sidered in the study as a one-year survival classiﬁcation.
We selected the one-year survival threshold to align with
prior studies and clinical treatment windows. This binary
classiﬁcation provides a practical and interpretable clini-
cal milestone used in early treatment planning and patient
stratiﬁcation. Moreover, this choice enables robust evalua-
tion using standard classiﬁcation and survival stratiﬁcation
techniques. Examples of high and low risk images, and
visualization of extracted features from images and genes
can be found in Fig.1. The integration of genomic data
and histopathologic images, referred to as multi-modal data,
plays a crucial role in deciphering the heterogeneity and com-
plexity of cancer. Histopathological images provide critical
insights into tissue morphology, while genomic data reveal
molecular-level details that can indicate tumor behavior and
patient prognosis. The development of survival prediction
models [11, 34], that utilize multi-modal data, highlights
the value of combining complementary information from
different sources to improve prediction accuracy. Over the
past few years, deep learning-based multi-modal fusion has
emerged as a powerful interdisciplinary approach for solv-
ing complex prediction problems [21]. As a result, several
advanced fusion methods using deep learning have been
introduced for cancer survival prediction [36, 37], providing
high ﬂexibility in integrating heterogeneous data modali-
ties through nonlinear modeling. The primary limitation of
these studies is the absence of a thorough analysis based
on different types of fusion methods. Recent advancements
in multi-modal learning have signiﬁcantly enhanced cancer
survival prediction and subtype classiﬁcation by integrating
histopathology and genomic data. Liu et al. [30] introduce
a Mutual-Guided Cross-Modality Transformer (MGCT) that
utilizes cross-modalityattentiontoenhancesurvival outcome
prediction. However, the study is limited by the absence
of a public dataset and a comprehensive comparison of
different fusion methods. Similarly, Li et al. [28] utilize
cross-modal alignment and contrastive learning to improve
predictive accuracy. Farooq et al. [18] explore multi-modal
representation learning for lung cancer survival prediction,
achieving enhanced outcomes via uniﬁed feature represen-
tations. However, while the study incorporates multi-modal
data, it excludes histopathology images, focusing solely on
CT and PET images. Ding et al. [14] propose a co-attention
fusion network with online data augmentation for cancer
subtype classiﬁcation, highlighting adaptive feature integra-
tion. While the study addresses survival prediction, it is
constrained by its reliance on private datasets. Jia et al.
[24] propose a Deep Convolution Cascade Attention Fusion
Network (DCCAFN) for lung cancer survival analysis, high-
lighting hierarchical feature fusion.
Multi-modallearninghasproventobeapowerfulapproach
for integrating different data sources, but the optimal method
for fusing these modalities remains an open question.
Existing research has explored different fusion strategies,
which can be broadly categorized into the following: early
fusion, where the data modalities are combined at the
input level; intermediate fusion, where separate represen-
tations are learned and combined at a hidden layer; and
late fusion, where individual models are trained separately
and their predictions aggregated [29]. Each approach offers
unique advantages and challenges, inﬂuencing model per-
formance based on data characteristics and task complexity.
An overview of the three fusion categories is shown in
Fig.2. Early fusion leverages a combined feature space to
directly learn the relationships between modalities, poten-
tially capturing interactions from the outset. However, it risks
information loss due to high-dimensional feature spaces and
requires careful preprocessing [7, 9]. Intermediate fusion
balances the integration by learning separate modality-
speciﬁc representations before merging them, which can
capture modality-speciﬁc nuances while preserving cross-
modal interactions [26]. Late fusion capitalizes on fully
trained, independent models and combines their outputs,
which can be advantageous when modalities exhibit vary-
ing predictive strengths [33]. Despite advancements, few
studies have systematically evaluated fusion strategies for
breast cancer survival prediction [20, 27, 36, 37]. Ana-
lyzing performance on multi-modal datasets that combine
histopathologic and genomic data provides critical insights.
Table 1 highlights a comparison of existing methods and
identiﬁes research gaps, motivating our framework to address
these deﬁciencies.
In this study, we aim to address this gap by evaluating
early, intermediate, and late fusion models for breast cancer
survival prediction. We develop deep learning frameworks
tailored to each fusion strategy and assess their performance
using comprehensive metrics, including accuracy, F1-score,
and AUC. Additionally, we conduct survival analysis to val-
idate model outputs against real patient outcomes, offering a
holistic view of their predictive utility. We provide an empir-
ical evaluation to demonstrate the effectiveness of the fusion
methods, emphasizing their beneﬁts for breast cancer sur-
vival prediction. In this study, only two previous papers [36,
37] are referenced, and their results are compared here based
on a public multi-modal dataset. Moreover, integrating sta-
tistical survival analysis, such as Kaplan–Meier curves and
log-rank tests, enhances the interpretability and clinical rele-
vance of predictive models [35]. To advance methodological
innovation, we further introduce a hybrid combination strat-

egy that integrates fusion techniques within a uniﬁed model.
This approach is designed to explore the impact of the com-
bination on our results. We also perform SHAP (SHapley
Additive exPlanations [32])-based interpretability analysis
to quantify the contributions of imaging and genomic fea-
turesandimprovetransparencyofmodeldecisions.Toensure
full reproducibility, all code, model conﬁgurations, and pre-
processing scripts have been made publicly available. In
conclusion, our main contributions are as follows:
• This study provides an extensive evaluation of early,
intermediate, and late fusion methods for breast can-
cer survival prediction using multi-modal data from
histopathology images and genomics.
• We propose a hybrid fusion strategy that combines fusion
methods in a single model to explore the impact of the
combination.
• Our research includes thorough statistical analysis to
compare the efﬁcacy of different fusion approaches,
including survival validation using Kaplan–Meier anal-
ysis and log-rank tests.
• We introduce SHAP-based interpretability to identify
modality-speciﬁc contributions and the most inﬂuential
genomic and imaging features in survival prediction.
• We identify key strengths and limitations of each fusion
category and propose potential pathways for future
improvements in multi-modal survival prediction mod-
els.
Methodology
This study aims to evaluate and compare various fusion
strategies for integrating histopathology image features and
genomic data in breast cancer survival prediction. The meth-
ods are categorized into early, intermediate, and late fusion
strategies. Each category comprises a set of methods, some of
which have been explored in this study. Figure3 shows the
top-performing methods for each category, along with the
combination strategy that integrates these three approaches.
Early fusion methods
Early fusion refers to combining raw features from differ-
ent modalities at the input level, enabling the model to learn
cross-modal interactions from the beginning. This section
discusses existing methods and potential future enhance-
ments:
Concatenation-based early fusion
This method involves directly concatenating histopathology
image features and genomic features before feeding them
into a deep learning model. Although simple, it can cap-
ture basic interactions but may suffer from high-dimensional
input space, potentially leading to overﬁtting.
Feature embedding with dimensionality reduction
To address the high dimensionality, early fusion can be
combined with techniques like principal component anal-
ysis (PCA) or autoencoders to reduce feature space before
fusion. This approach helps mitigate the risk of overﬁtting
while retaining essential information. In our implementa-
tion, after concatenating these streams into a single vector,
autoencoders are used for dimensionality reduction and
transformation, and a ﬁnal dense layer is used for classiﬁ-
cation. The model was implemented by adapting principles
from multi-modal learning and feature integration through an
autoencoder commonly found in biomedical machine learn-
ing workﬂows [15, 43].
Early attention mechanisms
Implementing an attention mechanism early in the network
allows the model to focus on the most relevant cross-
modalfeatures.Thiscanenhanceperformancebyprioritizing
important interactions between image and genomic data.
Attention is applied after initial feature extraction to enhance
modality-speciﬁc features. This process assigns weights to
features based on their relevance, allowing the network to
focus on the most informative elements. Using techniques
like self-attention or softmax-based weighting, each modal-
ity’s features are enhanced independently. The attended
features from both modalities are then concatenated and
passed through fully connected layers for classiﬁcation [8,
41].
Hybrid CNN–MLP models
A hybrid model combining convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) for image feature extraction and multi-layer percep-
trons (MLPs) for genomic data can be designed to merge
features at an early stage. This ensures that modality-speciﬁc
processing is maintained while leveraging combined fea-
tures. In our implementation, the Hybrid CNN–MLP Model
combines a 1D CNN for features extracted from imaging data
and an MLP for genomics data. The CNN extracts spatial
features using convolutional layers with ReLU activations,
while the MLP processes genomic features via dense lay-
ers. Outputs from both are concatenated for feature fusion,
followed by fully connected layers for classiﬁcation [38].

Intermediate fusion methods
Intermediate fusion methods involve processing each data
modality separately to create modality-speciﬁc representa-
tions, which are then combined at a hidden layer for further
learning.
Parallel CNN–MLP with fusion layer
Separate CNN and MLP branches are used to process image
andgenomicdata,respectively,withasharedfusionlayerthat
combinestheoutputsatanintermediatestage.Thisallowsthe
model to learn modality-speciﬁc features before integrating
them for joint learning. In the intermediate fusion method,
each modality is processed separately through its own dedi-
cated network, such as a CNN for image data and an MLP for
genomic data. After extracting features independently, these
modality-speciﬁc features are combined at an intermediate
stage. This allows the network to integrate complementary
information from both modalities before passing the fused
features through further layers for decision-making, offering
moreﬂexibilityandcapturingcomplexrelationshipsbetween
different data types [29].
Cross-attention mechanisms
Introducing cross-attention layers between modality-speciﬁc
branches allows the model to learn relationships between
different data types at deeper levels. This helps to reﬁne
the fusion process by selectively focusing on important
features. Cross-Attention Mechanisms enable intermediate
fusion by focusing on interactions between modalities at
speciﬁc feature representation levels. In this approach, two
feature sets (e.g., imaging and genomic) are processed sep-
arately by modality-speciﬁc encoders. Their outputs serve
as queries, keys, and values for a cross-attention module,
where features from one modality inﬂuence the representa-
tion of the other. This enhances the fused representation by
aligning complementary information. The resulting features
are concatenated or merged for downstream tasks, empha-
sizing interdependence between modalities while preserving
unique characteristics [44].
Residual fusion networks
Residual blocks are introduced in the intermediate fusion
stage to enhance learning by allowing gradient ﬂow through
deep networks and preventing vanishing gradients. This
approach ensures that learned representations are retained
while improving training stability. This model leverages
residual connections to enhance intermediate fusion in multi-
modal tasks. In our implementation, separate sub-networks
process each modality (e.g., genomics and imaging), fol-
lowed by a fusion layer. The key feature is the addition of
residual connections between the original features and the
fused representation, ensuring gradient ﬂow and maintaining
modality-speciﬁc information. This approach avoids the van-
ishing gradient problem, allows better feature propagation,
and facilitates the training of deeper architectures. By using
residuals, the network balances individual modality contri-
butions with the fused representation, making it effective for
integrating complex, multi-modal data [19].
Multi-modal transformer networks
Using transformers for intermediate fusion enables the
model to learn complex, long-range dependencies between
genomic and imaging features. This approach can capture
intricate interactions and improve interpretability through
attention weights. Multi-Modal Transformer Networks inte-
grate multiple modalities like imaging and genomics through
transformer-based mechanisms. Features are ﬁrst extracted
using CNNs for images and dense layers for genomic data.
These are then projected into a shared space, enabling trans-
formers to apply self-attention within each modality and
cross-attention across modalities. This captures intra- and
inter-modality dependencies dynamically. The fused features
are processed through classiﬁcation layers for the ﬁnal pre-
diction. This method excels at learning complex relationships
in multi-modal data and is inspired by foundational trans-
former work [41] and multi-modal extensions [40].
Late fusion methods
Late fusion involves training independent models for each
modality and combining their outputs for the ﬁnal prediction.
This strategy often beneﬁts from leveraging the strengths of
each modality independently before integrating predictions.
Ensemble of modal-speciﬁc models
Separate CNNs and MLPs are trained for histopathology and
genomic data. Their outputs are combined through an ensem-
ble strategy, such as averaging or majority voting, to generate
the ﬁnal prediction. This model involves training separate
models tailored for each modality (e.g., imaging, genomics)
independently. Each model is optimized for its speciﬁc input
type and feature space. During inference, the outputs of these
models (e.g., class probabilities) are combined using meth-
ods likeaveraging, weightedvoting, or meta-learners tomake
the ﬁnal prediction. This approach leverages the strengths of
each modality-speciﬁc model while maintaining modularity
and interpretability. It is particularly useful when modalities
are heterogeneous or have distinct structures that require dif-
ferent processing techniques [29].

Weighted fusion of outputs
Assigning learnable weights to the outputs of individual
models can enhance performance by prioritizing the more
informative modality during prediction. This approach is
dynamic and can adapt based on the reliability of each data
source. The method combines the predictions from multiple
modalities (e.g., imaging, genomics, and clinical data) by
assigning different weights to each modality’s output. The
weights can be learned or manually assigned based on the
importance of each modality in the given task. This approach
allows for a more ﬂexible integration of multi-modal infor-
mation, as it emphasizes the modalities that contribute more
to the predictive task and diminishes the inﬂuence of less
relevant modalities. The weighted fusion technique is espe-
cially useful in scenarios where different modalities exhibit
varying degrees of reliability or relevance, thereby improving
the overall prediction accuracy. Methods like these have been
explored in various applications, such as emotion recognition
and multimedia analysis [31], where they demonstrate the
efﬁcacy of weighted aggregation for performance enhance-
ment in multi-modal systems.
Meta-learning for fusion
Ameta-learnermodelcanbetrainedtolearnhowtobestcom-
bine the predictions from each modality-speciﬁc model. This
technique uses a higher-level network to optimize the com-
bination strategy and improve robustness. The model learns
how to effectively combine different modalities during the
training process, typically by learning a shared representa-
tion or mapping function that adapts based on the modality
characteristics. In this framework, meta-learning techniques
are applied to optimize the fusion of modalities, allowing the
model to learn the most effective way to combine them in
the context of speciﬁc tasks or datasets. This approach lever-
ages models like MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning)
or other meta-learning algorithms, where the objective is not
just to perform well on a speciﬁc task, but to generalize the
fusion process itself, improving performance on unseen tasks
with multiple modalities. Meta-learning for fusion has been
used in multi-modal classiﬁcation, disease prediction, and
other domains where different sources of data (e.g., imaging,
genomic, and clinical data) must be integrated into a single
model. The key advantage of this method is that it allows the
model to adaptively adjust its fusion strategy based on task-
speciﬁc requirements, thereby improving performance and
generalizability. This technique is closely related to studies
in the ﬁeld of transfer learning and few-shot learning, as it
emphasizes ﬂexibility and adaptability in how multi-modal
information is handled [5].
Stacked generalization (blending)
The outputs from modality-speciﬁc models are used as inputs
to a higher-level model, such as a logistic regression or small
neural network, to learn the best combination of predictions
for improved survival prediction. The model is an ensem-
ble learning method that combines the predictions of several
base models by training a new model to learn how to opti-
mally combine these outputs. It involves two main steps:
ﬁrst, multiple base models (such as decision trees, support
vector machines, or neural networks) are trained on the data,
each making predictions independently. Then, the second-
level model (often a simpler model like logistic regression
or a neural network) is trained on the predictions made by
the base models, essentially learning how to weigh or blend
them for the ﬁnal prediction. This approach helps to improve
model accuracy by leveraging the strengths of multiple mod-
els and mitigating their individual weaknesses. The output of
the second-level model is the ﬁnal prediction, which typically
performs better than any single base model. This technique
is widely used in machine learning competitions and real-
world applications where combining different models can
lead to superior performance, such as in Stacked Generaliza-
tion for classiﬁcation tasks, ensemble learning frameworks,
and multi-modal fusion problems [17].
Combination strategy: a hybrid fusion approach
To incorporate hybrid fusion approaches that leverage the
advantages of different models, we propose a combina-
tion strategy that integrates fusion methods. This hybrid
approach aims to overcome the limitations of individual
fusion strategies by combining their strengths, thus contribut-
ing to methodological innovation in the ﬁeld. Speciﬁcally, the
combination strategy processes imaging data through a CNN
and genomic data through an MLP, applies cross-attention
to capture inter-modal interactions, and uses a meta-learner
MLP to produce the ﬁnal classiﬁcation output. By integrating
these diverse techniques, the proposed method enhances the
robustness and accuracy of breast cancer survival prediction,
as detailed in Fig.3 (bottom right). Promising results from
this approach would harness the complementary strengths
of early (embedding-level), intermediate (cross-attention),
and late (meta-learning) fusion, while establishing a robust
framework for future advancements in multi-modal data inte-
gration.
Experimental settings
The survival prediction performance was assessed using
a multi-modal dataset consisting of histopathology images
and genomic proﬁles. Comprehensive evaluation metrics
included accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

30
Methods across the three fusion categories were compared,
with the meta-learning approach in the late fusion category
achieving the best performance across all metrics. Various
training scenarios were explored to enhance understanding.
Experiments were conducted on a 64-bit Windows 10 Pro
system, utilizing Python with the PyTorch framework, a CPU
E5-2650 v4 @ 2.20 GHz, 128 GB RAM, and four NVIDIA
GTX TITAN X GPUs.
Datasets
We evaluated the methods using publicly available breast
cancer data [36, 37] from TCGA-BRCA (The Cancer
Genome Atlas Breast Invasive Carcinoma), which con-
tains histopathology images and RNA-seq proﬁles for 1095
patients. A sample of the structure of the dataset is shown
in Fig.4. The histopathology images, obtained from the
National Cancer Institute’s Genomic Data Commons, are
high-resolution whole-slide images exceeding 20,000 ×
20,000 pixels. Nuclei segmentations for these images were
generated using an adversarial learning-based segmentation
model [22], and the segmentations were divided into patches
of 2000 × 2000 pixels to create segmentation masks for all
slides. From each whole-slide image, 25 non-overlapping
patches, each measuring 2000 × 2000 pixels, are randomly
selected to facilitate the extraction of imaging features. These
patches and their corresponding segmentation masks were
processed using the CellProﬁler software [6] to compute fea-
tures related to cell and nucleus intensity, shape, and texture.
Examples of extracted features include area, eccentricity,
compactness, Euler number, and Zernike moments. Stain-
speciﬁc intensity features were separately calculated for
hematoxylin (H) and eosin (E). A 5-bin histogram summa-
rization was performed for 215 extracted features, resulting
in a 1075-dimensional feature vector per patient. Nuclei seg-
mentation data was available for 978 patients, and 973 patient
samples were successfully processed through CellProﬁler,
forming the dataset used in this study without additional
ﬁltering. RNA-seq data for all TCGA-BRCA patients was
downloaded from the FireBrowse platform. Gene variabil-
ity was determined by calculating the coefﬁcient of variation
(σ/μ) for log2-transformed expression values. Genomic fea-
ture vectors were constructed by selecting the top 500, 800,
1000, and 3000 most variable genes, and their z-scores were
used for representation. Additionally, protein–protein inter-
action data from the STRING database was incorporated to
account for biological dependencies among genes, with inter-
action weights providing insights into their relationships.
For evaluation, we employed 5-fold cross-validation,
dividing the dataset into training (544 patients), valida-
tion (137 patients), and testing (292 patients) sets. Figure5
presents a summary of patient characteristics in the TCGA-
BRCA dataset.
Model architecture and training details
To ensure reproducibility, this subsection provides a com-
prehensive description of the model architectures, hyper-
parameters, and training procedures for the fusion strate-
gies evaluated in this study. The models were imple-
mented using PyTorch, with code available in a pub-
lic GitHub repository (https://anonymous.4open.science/r/
Breast-Cancer-Survival-940B/). The study evaluates multi-
ple fusion strategies, including early, intermediate, and late
fusion, each with distinct architectures tailored to integrate
genomic and imaging modalities. Below, we summarize the
key models: All models were trained with a consistent set of
hyperparameters to ensure fair comparison, unless otherwise
speciﬁed. The learning rate was set to 0.001 using the Adam
optimizer with a weight decay of 1 × 10−5 for regulariza-
tion. The batch size was 32, as implemented in the PyTorch
DataLoader. Training ran for up to 100 epochs, with
early stopping triggered if validation loss did not improve
for 10 consecutive epochs. Dropout was applied where
speciﬁed (e.g., 0.3 in ImprovedEarlyFusionModel). The
MultiModalTransformerFusion model used a transformer-
speciﬁc dropout rate of 0.1. Binary cross-entropy was used
as the loss function for all models. Table 2 summarizes the
architectures, hyperparameters, and training details for each
model, providing a concise reference for reproducibility.
Results and discussion
In this study, we applied multi-modal approaches in early,
intermediate and late fusion to predict breast cancer survival
using a combination of imaging data and genomic features.
The performance of the proposed model was evaluated using
several standard metrics, AUC, accuracy, precision, recall,
and F1-score as shown in Tables 3, 4, and 5 and Fig.12. A
Kaplan–Meier survival analysis was also performed for the
statistical analysis, as shown in Figs.6, 7, and 8 and Table 6.
Early fusion strategies
Table 3 presents that comprehensive evaluation results for
early fusion methods across ﬁve key metrics. Feature Embed-
ding with Dimensionality Reduction emerges as the superior
approach, achieving the highest accuracy (63.01%), F1-score
(67.27%), and AUC (63.11%). This method’s superior per-
formance can be attributed to its ability to effectively reduce
redundancy while capturing critical feature interactions. In
contrast, Concatenation-Based Fusion, while achieving the
highest recall (83.45%), exhibited lower accuracy and AUC
values, indicating potential overﬁtting to certain patterns.
Figure6 shows that Kaplan–Meier survival analysis fur-
ther highlighted the effectiveness of early fusion methods.

Intermediate fusion strategies
Table 4 shows that among intermediate fusion approaches,
the Parallel CNN–MLP with Fusion Layer emerged as the
top performer, with the highest accuracy (63.70%), precision
(60.43%), and F1-score (68.07%). This approach leverages
complementary features by parallel processing, enhancing
its overall predictive accuracy. In contrast, Cross-Attention
Mechanisms underperformed across all metrics, achieving
an AUC of only 49.36%.
Figure7 and Table 6 show that survival analysis corrob-
orated these ﬁndings. While the Parallel CNN–MLP with
Fusion Layer and Residual Fusion Networks (Fig.7c, d)
produced statistically signiﬁcant differences in survival dis-
tributions (p < 0.05), Multi-Modal Transformer Networks
(Fig.7b) failed to differentiate between high- and low-risk
groups (p = 0.987). These results suggest that intermediate
fusion strategies are sensitive to architectural choices, with
simple, parallel designs outperforming more complex mech-
anisms.
Late fusion strategies
Table 5 shows that late fusion methods consistently demon-
strated strong performance, with Meta-Learning for Fusion
achieving the best results (Accuracy: 64.38%, F1-score:
68.10%,AUC:64.47%).Thisapproach’sabilitytoadaptively
weight model outputs likely contributed to its effectiveness in
handling multi-modal data. While Weighted Fusion of Out-
puts and Stacked Ensemble Learning achieved comparable
results, they were slightly less effective than meta-learning
methods.
Kaplan–Meier analysis (Fig.8; Table 6) revealed that all
late fusion methods, except Weighted Fusion of Outputs
(p < 0.0001), showed statistically signiﬁcant survival pre-
diction capabilities (p < 0.01). The success of these methods
emphasizes the importance of robust aggregation strategies
when fusing independent model predictions.
Combination strategy performance
The proposed combination strategy, which integrates fusion
methods, was evaluated for its effectiveness in integrat-
ing histopathology image features and genomic data for
breast cancer survival prediction. The results are as fol-
lows: Accuracy: 0.6233, Precision: 0.6036, Recall: 0.7034,
F1 Score: 0.6497, AUC: 0.6238, and a Log-rank Test p-value
of 0.000386, indicating a statistically signiﬁcant difference
in survival distributions. Although these metrics did not
outperform the top methods in individual categories, the
Kaplan–Meier survival curves in Fig.9 demonstrate a clear
separation between predicted survival groups (>1 year vs.
≤1year),highlightingthestrategy’sabilitytodistinguishsur-
vival outcomes. This hybrid approach provides a foundation
for future optimization, such as reﬁning the cross-attention
mechanism or enhancing the meta-learner, to potentially
improve performance.

Comparative insights
Overall, the results indicate that late fusion methods, partic-
ularly Meta-Learning for Fusion, outperform early, interme-
diate, and hybrid fusion strategies in breast cancer survival
prediction, as shown in Fig.12. Early fusion methods ben-
eﬁt from simplicity but are often constrained by feature
redundancy. Intermediate fusion methods, while effective
in some cases, are sensitive to design choices and require

In our experiments, some of the more complex architec-
tures, such as the Multi-Modal Transformer Networks and
Cross-Attention Mechanisms, underperformed compared to
simpler fusion strategies. We believe there are a few key fac-
tors contributing to this outcome. Data Insufﬁciency: Despite
using over 900 patient samples from TCGA-BRCA, the
sample size remains relatively small for training deep and
highly parameterized models like transformers or attention-
based architectures. These models typically require much
larger datasets to generalize effectively. Overﬁtting: The high
capacity of these models likely led to overﬁtting on train-
ing data. While we applied basic regularization (dropout
and weight decay), more advanced strategies such as early
stopping, stronger regularizers, or data augmentation for
genomic and imaging features were not fully explored due to
resource constraints. We will consider these in future itera-
tions. Lack of Pretraining: We did not use pretrained weights
for the transformer components or attention modules. Given
the limited data, initializing these models from pretrained
representations, especially on related tasks, could signif-
icantly improve performance. We acknowledge important
limitations in our statistical evaluation approach. While we
employed 5-fold cross-validation and reported mean perfor-

mance metrics, we did not perform formal statistical testing
(ANOVA or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests) to compare perfor-
mance differences between fusion methods. Without such
paired statistical tests, we cannot deﬁnitively claim statistical
signiﬁcance of performance differences between methods.
However, the consistent ranking of methods across mul-
tiple evaluation metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) and the
statistically signiﬁcant survival stratiﬁcation provide reason-
able conﬁdence in our comparative conclusions. Future work
will implement paired statistical testing by storing individual
fold results to enable rigorous inter-method comparisons.
Implications and future directions
The results highlight the effectiveness of multi-modal fusion
in predicting breast cancer survival, with late fusion showing
signiﬁcant promise. Nonetheless, performance inconsisten-
cies across approaches emphasize the necessity for deeper
investigation into optimizing architectures and integration
methods.Subsequentresearchcouldexploreadvancedfusion
strategies, such as dynamic weighting or graph-based tech-
niques, to boost both precision and interpretability. Expand-
ing dataset size and diversity may also improve model
applicability in clinical settings. Future work could incor-
porate additional data types, such as clinical information
(e.g., hormone receptor status, treatment details), radiomics,
or longitudinal data, to enhance generalizability. While this
study treats survival prediction as a binary classiﬁcation
task, future efforts might adopt time-to-event frameworks
like DeepSurv or CoxPH-based neural networks for contin-
uous risk assessment. Additionally, pretraining large models
on related histopathology datasets and leveraging domain-
adaptive transformers could further enhance performance on
smaller biomedical datasets.
Discussion and conclusions
In this study, we applied multi-modal approaches across
early, intermediate, late, and hybrid fusion strategies to pre-
dict breast cancer survival using combined histopathology
and genomic features. The results, presented in Tables 3, 4,
and 5, show that late fusion methods consistently achieve
higher performance across all major evaluation metrics.
Among them, the meta-learning-based late fusion approach
yields the best overall results, with the highest accuracy
(64.38%), F1-score (68.10%), and AUC (64.47%). Addi-
tionally, survival stratiﬁcation using Kaplan–Meier analysis
and log-rank tests (Table 6) demonstrates that this method
achieves statistically signiﬁcant separation of high- and
low-risk groups, further reinforcing its effectiveness. We
introduced a hybrid fusion method that combines fusion
methods into a uniﬁed architecture. This combination strat-
egy processes histopathology data via a CNN, genomic
data via an MLP, incorporates a cross-attention mechanism
to capture inter-modal relationships, and concludes with a
meta-learning classiﬁer. This hybrid approach, illustrated in
Fig.3 and detailed in section “Combination strategy: a hybrid
fusion approach”. We implemented a comprehensive eval-
uation framework to compare these fusion methods using
standard classiﬁcation metrics and survival analysis. While
the comparisons between individual methods are detailed
within each fusion category, we also provide a visual sum-
mary in Fig.12 that presents the best-performing method
from each category side by side. This comparison, com-
bined with the full set of classiﬁcation metrics and survival
analyses, offers a comprehensive and interpretable basis
for concluding that the late fusion strategy is the most
robust and reliable in this context. To enhance interpretabil-
ity, we performed a SHAP feature importance analysis on
the hybrid model. This analysis revealed that genomic fea-
tures contribute approximately 72–73% and imaging features
28–29% to the model’s predictions, with both modalities
offering complementary insights. Figures10 and 11 visualize
the global and individual feature importances, respectively,
providing transparency into the model’s decision-making
process and aiding clinical interpretability.
Kaplan–Meier survival analysis revealed statistically sig-
niﬁcant differences in survival distributions for most fusion
methods, with notable exceptions such as Hybrid CNN–MLP
Models and Multi-Modal Transformer Networks, which
lacked statistical signiﬁcance. We acknowledge that multi-
ple log-rank tests were performed across different methods.
Although we did not apply formal correction methods (e.g.,
Bonferroni [16] or False Discovery Rate (FDR) [4]), most
of the signiﬁcant results have p-values well below the con-
ventional threshold (e.g., p < 0.01), suggesting that the
conclusions remain robust. This limitation is noted, and
future work may incorporate correction strategies to further
strengthen statistical inference. As a classical baseline, we
compared our methods with the sparse canonical correla-
tion analysis (CCA)-based fusion method by Subramanian
et al. [36], which represents a non-deep-learning approach
to multi-modal survival prediction. The results, included in
Table 4, show that other intermediate fusion models outper-
form this classical fusion baseline, highlighting the beneﬁt of
modality-aware representation learning in this context. The
relatively lower performance of more complex models, such
as the Multi-Modal Transformer and Cross-Attention-based
networks, may be attributed to overﬁtting due to limited train-
ing data. These models typically require larger datasets and
beneﬁt from pretraining and advanced regularization, which
were not applied in this study. This observation highlights
the trade-off between model complexity and generalization
in the context of multi-modal biomedical data and under-

One limitation of our study is the absence of other datasets.
While METABRIC [13] provides genomic and clinical data,
it lacks matched histopathology images, which restricts its
suitability for validating our fusion models. Future work
will focus on identifying or assembling datasets that include
both modalities to enable external validation of multi-modal
approaches and assess generalizability.
Overall, this study presents a comprehensive analysis of
multi-modal fusion strategies for breast cancer survival pre-
diction,leveragingearly,intermediate,late,andhybridfusion
approaches. Our results demonstrate that the choice of fusion
strategy should be informed by task complexity, modality
interaction, and data characteristics. The hybrid combination
method introduced here offers a ﬂexible and interpretable
framework that integrates complementary fusion principles,
achieving both high predictive accuracy and robust sur-
vival stratiﬁcation. All code and model conﬁgurations are
publicly available, supporting reproducibility and further
research. Future directions include integrating additional
data types (e.g., clinical variables, radiomics), reﬁning model
interpretability, and advancing toward clinical translation in
precision oncology.

chunks: Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12:30 https://doi.org/10.1007/s40747-025-02133-y ORIGINAL PAPER Integrating histopathology and genomic data: a comparative study of fusion methods for breast cancer survival prediction Younes Akbari1 · Faseela Abdullakutty1 · Somaya Al Maadeed1 · Ahmed Bouridane2,3 · Rifat Hamoudi3,4,5 Received: 7 March 2025 / Accepted: 1 October 2025 / Published online: 2 December 2025 © The Author(s) 2025 Abstract Accurate breast cancer survival prediction using multi-modal data is vital for enhancing clinical decisions. This study evaluates deep learning based fusion strategies, early, intermediate, late, and a hybrid approach, to integrate histopathology images and genomic data for one year survival prediction. We developed a robust evaluation framework, employing tailored deep learning architectures and metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC. Model performance was validated using Kaplan–Meier curves and log-rank tests, with SHAP-based feature importance analysis enhancing interpretability. Results highlight the strengths and limitations of each fusion strategy, offering insights into optimal multi-modal learning approaches for breast cancer prognosis. Our ﬁndings underscore the importance of selecting task speciﬁc fusion methods, providing a reproducible, interpretable framework to advance survival prediction. All code and conﬁgurations are publicly available. Keywords Histopathology image · Genomic data · Fusion methods · Breast cancer survival prediction Arabic Keywordsاﻟﺗﻧﺑؤ ﺑﺑﻘﺎء ﻣرﺿﻰﺳرطﺎن اﻟﺛدي·أﺳﺎﻟﯾب اﻟدﻣﺞ·اﻟﺑﯾﺎﻧﺎت اﻟﺟﯾﻧﯾﺔ·ﺻور اﻷﻧﺳﺟﺔاﻟﻣرﺿﯾﺔ B Younes Akbari younes.akbari@qu.edu.qa Faseela Abdullakutty faseela.abdullakutty@qu.edu.qa Somaya Al Maadeed s_alali@qu.edu.qa Ahmed Bouridane abouridane@sharjah.ac.ae Rifat Hamoudi rhamoudi@sharjah.ac.ae 1 Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Qatar University, Doha, Qatar 2 Center for Data Analytics and Cybernetics, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates 3 BIMAI-Lab, Biomedically Informed Artiﬁcial Intelligence Laboratory, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates 4 Research Institute for Medical and Health Sciences, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates 5 Clinical Sciences Department, College of Medicine, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates Introduction Breastcancerisoneofthemostprevalentandlife-threatening diseases worldwide, and accurate survival prediction plays a pivotal role in guiding treatment strategies and improving patient outcomes [23, 25]. The prediction of patient survival is usually understood as a task of survival time regres- sion modeling [12]. The Cox proportional hazards (CoxPH) model is a widely used approach to survival prediction in which a linear combination of variables determines the log- partial risk for a patient [39]. In addition, several extensions of the CoxPH model, including LASSO regularization [39] and deep neural networks trained with Cox partial likeli- hood loss [12, 27], have achieved remarkable success and promising results. Almost all Cox regression studies employ only genomic data for survival prediction. Molecular data can provide important insights into cancer patient survival; how- ever, integrating additional cancer-related information could improve predictive accuracy. In addition to genomic data, pathologic images reveal cellular morphologic features that are closely associated with patient survival [1, 3]. The advent of digital whole-slide imaging has enabled computer-assisted techniques for analyzing pathological images, which greatly improve efﬁciency, accuracy, and consistency [2, 11]. There- 123 30 Page 2 of 20 Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12 :30 fore, various image-based approaches [10, 35] have been developed for survival prediction. These methods extract fea- tures from only pathological images, such as size, shape, distribution and texture of cell nuclei, which have shown a strong correlation with survival outcome and pave the way for further research opportunities. In general, these images can be categorized into high and low risk, which we con- sidered in the study as a one-year survival classiﬁcation. We selected the one-year survival threshold to align with prior studies and clinical treatment windows. This binary classiﬁcation provides a practical and interpretable clini- cal milestone used in early treatment planning and patient stratiﬁcation. Moreover, this choice enables robust evalua- tion using standard classiﬁcation and survival stratiﬁcation techniques. Examples of high and low risk images, and visualization of extracted features from images and genes can be found in Fig.1. The integration of genomic data and histopathologic images, referred to as multi-modal data, plays a crucial role in deciphering the heterogeneity and com- plexity of cancer. Histopathological images provide critical insights into tissue morphology, while genomic data reveal molecular-level details that can indicate tumor behavior and patient prognosis. The development of survival prediction models [11, 34], that utilize multi-modal data, highlights the value of combining complementary information from different sources to improve prediction accuracy. Over the past few years, deep learning-based multi-modal fusion has emerged as a powerful interdisciplinary approach for solv- ing complex prediction problems [21]. As a result, several advanced fusion methods using deep learning have been introduced for cancer survival prediction [36, 37], providing high ﬂexibility in integrating heterogeneous data modali-

of cancer. Histopathological images provide critical insights into tissue morphology, while genomic data reveal molecular-level details that can indicate tumor behavior and patient prognosis. The development of survival prediction models [11, 34], that utilize multi-modal data, highlights the value of combining complementary information from different sources to improve prediction accuracy. Over the past few years, deep learning-based multi-modal fusion has emerged as a powerful interdisciplinary approach for solv- ing complex prediction problems [21]. As a result, several advanced fusion methods using deep learning have been introduced for cancer survival prediction [36, 37], providing high ﬂexibility in integrating heterogeneous data modali- ties through nonlinear modeling. The primary limitation of these studies is the absence of a thorough analysis based on different types of fusion methods. Recent advancements in multi-modal learning have signiﬁcantly enhanced cancer survival prediction and subtype classiﬁcation by integrating histopathology and genomic data. Liu et al. [30] introduce a Mutual-Guided Cross-Modality Transformer (MGCT) that utilizes cross-modalityattentiontoenhancesurvival outcome prediction. However, the study is limited by the absence of a public dataset and a comprehensive comparison of different fusion methods. Similarly, Li et al. [28] utilize cross-modal alignment and contrastive learning to improve predictive accuracy. Farooq et al. [18] explore multi-modal representation learning for lung cancer survival prediction, achieving enhanced outcomes via uniﬁed feature represen- tations. However, while the study incorporates multi-modal data, it excludes histopathology images, focusing solely on CT and PET images. Ding et al. [14] propose a co-attention fusion network with online data augmentation for cancer subtype classiﬁcation, highlighting adaptive feature integra- tion. While the study addresses survival prediction, it is constrained by its reliance on private datasets. Jia et al. [24] propose a Deep Convolution Cascade Attention Fusion Network (DCCAFN) for lung cancer survival analysis, high- lighting hierarchical feature fusion. Multi-modallearninghasproventobeapowerfulapproach for integrating different data sources, but the optimal method for fusing these modalities remains an open question. Existing research has explored different fusion strategies, which can be broadly categorized into the following: early fusion, where the data modalities are combined at the input level; intermediate fusion, where separate represen- tations are learned and combined at a hidden layer; and late fusion, where individual models are trained separately and their predictions aggregated [29]. Each approach offers unique advantages and challenges, inﬂuencing model per- formance based on data characteristics and task complexity. An overview of the three fusion categories is shown in Fig.2. Early fusion leverages a combined feature space to directly learn the relationships between modalities, poten- tially capturing interactions from the outset. However, it risks information loss due to high-dimensional feature spaces and requires careful preprocessing [7, 9]. Intermediate fusion balances the integration by learning separate modality- speciﬁc representations before merging them, which can capture modality-speciﬁc nuances while preserving cross- modal interactions [26]. Late fusion capitalizes on fully trained, independent models and combines their outputs, which can be advantageous when modalities exhibit vary- ing predictive strengths [33]. Despite advancements, few studies have systematically evaluated fusion strategies for breast cancer survival prediction [20, 27, 36, 37]. Ana- lyzing performance on multi-modal datasets that combine histopathologic and genomic data provides critical insights. Table 1 highlights a comparison of existing methods and identiﬁes research gaps, motivating our framework to address these deﬁciencies. In this study, we aim to address this gap by evaluating early, intermediate, and late fusion models for breast cancer survival prediction. We develop deep learning frameworks tailored to each fusion strategy and assess their performance using comprehensive metrics, including accuracy, F1-score, and AUC. Additionally, we conduct survival analysis to val- idate model outputs against real patient outcomes, offering a holistic view of their predictive utility. We provide an empir- ical evaluation to demonstrate the effectiveness of the fusion methods, emphasizing their beneﬁts for breast cancer sur- vival prediction. In this study, only two previous papers [36, 37] are referenced, and their results are compared here based on a public multi-modal dataset. Moreover, integrating sta- tistical survival analysis, such as Kaplan–Meier curves and log-rank tests, enhances the interpretability and clinical rele- vance of predictive models [35]. In conclusion, our main contributions are as follows: • This study provides an extensive evaluation of early, intermediate, and late fusion methods for breast can- cer survival prediction using multi-modal data from histopathology images and genomics. • We propose a hybrid fusion strategy that combines fusion methods in a single model to explore the impact of the combination. • Our research includes thorough statistical analysis to compare the efﬁcacy of different fusion approaches, including survival validation using Kaplan–Meier anal- ysis and log-rank tests. • We introduce SHAP-based interpretability to identify modality-speciﬁc contributions and the most inﬂuential genomic and imaging features in survival prediction. • We identify key strengths and limitations of each fusion category and propose potential pathways for future improvements in multi-modal survival prediction mod- els. Methodology This study aims to evaluate and compare various fusion strategies for integrating histopathology image features and genomic data in breast cancer survival prediction. The meth- ods are categorized into early, intermediate, and late fusion strategies. Each category comprises a set of methods, some of which have been explored in this study. Figure3 shows the top-performing methods for each category, along with the combination strategy that integrates these three approaches. Early fusion refers to combining raw features from differ- ent modalities at the input level, enabling the model to learn cross-modal interactions from the beginning. This section discusses existing methods and potential future enhance- ments: Concatenation-based early fusion This method involves directly concatenating histopathology image features and genomic features before feeding them into a deep learning model. Although simple, it can cap- ture basic interactions but may suffer from high-dimensional input space, potentially leading to overﬁtting. Feature embedding with dimensionality reduction To address the high dimensionality, early fusion can be combined with techniques like principal component anal- ysis (PCA) or autoencoders to reduce feature space before fusion. This approach helps mitigate the risk of overﬁtting while retaining essential information. In our implementa- tion, after concatenating these streams into a single vector, autoencoders are used for dimensionality reduction and transformation, and a ﬁnal dense layer is used for classiﬁ- cation. The model was implemented by adapting principles from multi-modal learning and feature integration through an autoencoder commonly found in biomedical machine learn- ing workﬂows [15, 43]. Early attention mechanisms Implementing an attention mechanism early in the network allows the model to focus on the most relevant cross- modalfeatures.Thiscanenhanceperformancebyprioritizing important interactions between image and genomic data. Attention is applied after initial feature extraction to enhance modality-speciﬁc features. This process assigns weights to features based on their relevance, allowing the network to focus on the most informative elements. Using techniques like self-attention or softmax-based weighting, each modal- ity’s features are enhanced independently. The attended features from both modalities are then concatenated and passed through fully connected layers for classiﬁcation [8, 41]. Hybrid CNN–MLP models A hybrid model combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for image feature extraction and multi-layer percep- trons (MLPs) for genomic data can be designed to merge features at an early stage. This ensures that modality-speciﬁc processing is maintained while leveraging combined fea- tures. In our implementation, the Hybrid CNN–MLP Model combines a 1D CNN for features extracted from imaging data and an MLP for genomics data. The CNN extracts spatial features using convolutional layers with ReLU activations, while the MLP processes genomic features via dense lay- ers. Outputs from both are concatenated for feature fusion, followed by fully connected layers for classiﬁcation [38]. 123 30 Page 6 of 20 Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12 :30 Fig. 3 Illustration of four fusion methods for integrating imaging (xi ∈Rdi ) and genomics (xg ∈Rdg) data to produce a classiﬁcation output (y ∈Rnc, nc = 2). Top right Parallel CNN–MLP: Imaging data is processed by a CNN, genomics by an MLP. Imaging data is processed by a CNN, genomics by an MLP, cross-attention is applied, features are concatenated, and a meta-learner MLP produces y 123 Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12 :30 Page 7 of 20 30 Intermediate fusion methods Intermediate fusion methods involve processing each data modality separately to create modality-speciﬁc representa- tions, which are then combined at a hidden layer for further learning. Parallel CNN–MLP with fusion layer Separate CNN and MLP branches are used to process image andgenomicdata,respectively,withasharedfusionlayerthat combinestheoutputsatanintermediatestage.Thisallowsthe model to learn modality-speciﬁc features before integrating them for joint learning. In the intermediate fusion method, each modality is processed separately through its own dedi- cated network, such as a CNN for image data and an MLP for genomic data. After extracting features independently, these modality-speciﬁc features are combined at an intermediate stage. This allows the network to integrate complementary information from both modalities before passing the fused features through further layers for decision-making, offering moreﬂexibilityandcapturingcomplexrelationshipsbetween different data types [29]. Cross-attention mechanisms Introducing cross-attention layers between modality-speciﬁc branches allows the model to learn relationships between different data types at deeper levels. This helps to reﬁne the fusion process by selectively focusing on important features. Cross-Attention Mechanisms enable intermediate fusion by focusing on interactions between modalities at speciﬁc feature representation levels. In this approach, two feature sets (e.g., imaging and genomic) are processed sep- arately by modality-speciﬁc encoders. Their outputs serve as queries, keys, and values for a cross-attention module, where features from one modality

before passing the fused features through further layers for decision-making, offering moreﬂexibilityandcapturingcomplexrelationshipsbetween different data types [29]. Cross-attention mechanisms Introducing cross-attention layers between modality-speciﬁc branches allows the model to learn relationships between different data types at deeper levels. This helps to reﬁne the fusion process by selectively focusing on important features. Cross-Attention Mechanisms enable intermediate fusion by focusing on interactions between modalities at speciﬁc feature representation levels. In this approach, two feature sets (e.g., imaging and genomic) are processed sep- arately by modality-speciﬁc encoders. Their outputs serve as queries, keys, and values for a cross-attention module, where features from one modality inﬂuence the representa- tion of the other. This enhances the fused representation by aligning complementary information. The resulting features are concatenated or merged for downstream tasks, empha- sizing interdependence between modalities while preserving unique characteristics [44]. Residual fusion networks Residual blocks are introduced in the intermediate fusion stage to enhance learning by allowing gradient ﬂow through deep networks and preventing vanishing gradients. This approach ensures that learned representations are retained while improving training stability. This model leverages residual connections to enhance intermediate fusion in multi- modal tasks. In our implementation, separate sub-networks process each modality (e.g., genomics and imaging), fol- lowed by a fusion layer. The key feature is the addition of residual connections between the original features and the fused representation, ensuring gradient ﬂow and maintaining modality-speciﬁc information. This approach avoids the van- ishing gradient problem, allows better feature propagation, and facilitates the training of deeper architectures. By using residuals, the network balances individual modality contri- butions with the fused representation, making it effective for integrating complex, multi-modal data [19]. Multi-modal transformer networks Using transformers for intermediate fusion enables the model to learn complex, long-range dependencies between genomic and imaging features. This approach can capture intricate interactions and improve interpretability through attention weights. Multi-Modal Transformer Networks inte- grate multiple modalities like imaging and genomics through transformer-based mechanisms. Features are ﬁrst extracted using CNNs for images and dense layers for genomic data. These are then projected into a shared space, enabling trans- formers to apply self-attention within each modality and cross-attention across modalities. This captures intra- and inter-modality dependencies dynamically. The fused features are processed through classiﬁcation layers for the ﬁnal pre- diction. This method excels at learning complex relationships in multi-modal data and is inspired by foundational trans- former work [41] and multi-modal extensions [40]. Late fusion methods Late fusion involves training independent models for each modality and combining their outputs for the ﬁnal prediction. This strategy often beneﬁts from leveraging the strengths of each modality independently before integrating predictions. Ensemble of modal-speciﬁc models Separate CNNs and MLPs are trained for histopathology and genomic data. Their outputs are combined through an ensem- ble strategy, such as averaging or majority voting, to generate the ﬁnal prediction. This model involves training separate models tailored for each modality (e.g., imaging, genomics) independently. Each model is optimized for its speciﬁc input type and feature space. During inference, the outputs of these models (e.g., class probabilities) are combined using meth- ods likeaveraging, weightedvoting, or meta-learners tomake the ﬁnal prediction. This approach leverages the strengths of each modality-speciﬁc model while maintaining modularity and interpretability. It is particularly useful when modalities are heterogeneous or have distinct structures that require dif- ferent processing techniques [29]. 123 30 Page 8 of 20 Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12 :30 Weighted fusion of outputs Assigning learnable weights to the outputs of individual models can enhance performance by prioritizing the more informative modality during prediction. This approach is dynamic and can adapt based on the reliability of each data source. The method combines the predictions from multiple modalities (e.g., imaging, genomics, and clinical data) by assigning different weights to each modality’s output. The weights can be learned or manually assigned based on the importance of each modality in the given task. This approach allows for a more ﬂexible integration of multi-modal infor- mation, as it emphasizes the modalities that contribute more to the predictive task and diminishes the inﬂuence of less relevant modalities. The weighted fusion technique is espe- cially useful in scenarios where different modalities exhibit varying degrees of reliability or relevance, thereby improving the overall prediction accuracy. Methods like these have been explored in various applications, such as emotion recognition and multimedia analysis [31], where they demonstrate the efﬁcacy of weighted aggregation for performance enhance- ment in multi-modal systems. multi-modal infor- mation, as it emphasizes the modalities that contribute more to the predictive task and diminishes the inﬂuence of less relevant modalities. The weighted fusion technique is espe- cially useful in scenarios where different modalities exhibit varying degrees of reliability or relevance, thereby improving the overall prediction accuracy. Methods like these have been explored in various applications, such as emotion recognition and multimedia analysis [31], where they demonstrate the efﬁcacy of weighted aggregation for performance enhance- ment in multi-modal systems. Meta-learning for fusion Ameta-learnermodelcanbetrainedtolearnhowtobestcom- bine the predictions from each modality-speciﬁc model. This technique uses a higher-level network to optimize the com- bination strategy and improve robustness. The model learns how to effectively combine different modalities during the training process, typically by learning a shared representa- tion or mapping function that adapts based on the modality characteristics. In this framework, meta-learning techniques are applied to optimize the fusion of modalities, allowing the model to learn the most effective way to combine them in the context of speciﬁc tasks or datasets. This approach lever- ages models like MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) or other meta-learning algorithms, where the objective is not just to perform well on a speciﬁc task, but to generalize the fusion process itself, improving performance on unseen tasks with multiple modalities. Meta-learning for fusion has been used in multi-modal classiﬁcation, disease prediction, and other domains where different sources of data (e.g., imaging, genomic, and clinical data) must be integrated into a single model. The key advantage of this method is that it allows the model to adaptively adjust its fusion strategy based on task- speciﬁc requirements, thereby improving performance and generalizability. This technique is closely related to studies in the ﬁeld of transfer learning and few-shot learning, as it emphasizes ﬂexibility and adaptability in how multi-modal information is handled [5]. Stacked generalization (blending) The outputs from modality-speciﬁc models are used as inputs to a higher-level model, such as a logistic regression or small neural network, to learn the best combination of predictions for improved survival prediction. The model is an ensem- ble learning method that combines the predictions of several base models by training a new model to learn how to opti- mally combine these outputs. It involves two main steps: ﬁrst, multiple base models (such as decision trees, support vector machines, or neural networks) are trained on the data, each making predictions independently. Then, the second- level model (often a simpler model like logistic regression or a neural network) is trained on the predictions made by the base models, essentially learning how to weigh or blend them for the ﬁnal prediction. This approach helps to improve model accuracy by leveraging the strengths of multiple mod- els and mitigating their individual weaknesses. The output of the second-level model is the ﬁnal prediction, which typically performs better than any single base model. This technique is widely used in machine learning competitions and real- world applications where combining different models can lead to superior performance, such as in Stacked Generaliza- tion for classiﬁcation tasks, ensemble learning frameworks, and multi-modal fusion problems [17]. Combination strategy: a hybrid fusion approach To incorporate hybrid fusion approaches that leverage the advantages of different models, we propose a combina- tion strategy that integrates fusion methods. This hybrid approach aims to overcome the limitations of individual fusion strategies by combining their strengths, thus contribut- ing to methodological innovation in the ﬁeld. Speciﬁcally, the combination strategy processes imaging data through a CNN and genomic data through an MLP, applies cross-attention to capture inter-modal interactions, and uses a meta-learner MLP to produce the ﬁnal classiﬁcation output. By integrating these diverse techniques, the proposed method enhances the robustness and accuracy of breast cancer survival prediction, as detailed in Fig.3 (bottom right). Promising results from this approach would harness the complementary strengths of early (embedding-level), intermediate (cross-attention), and late (meta-learning) fusion, while establishing a robust framework for future advancements in multi-modal data inte- gration. Datasets We evaluated the methods using publicly available breast cancer data [36, 37] from TCGA-BRCA (The Cancer Genome Atlas Breast Invasive Carcinoma), which con- tains histopathology images and RNA-seq proﬁles for 1095 patients. A sample of the structure of the dataset is shown in Fig.4. The histopathology images, obtained from the National Cancer Institute’s Genomic Data Commons, are high-resolution whole-slide images exceeding 20,000 × 20,000 pixels. Nuclei segmentations for these images were generated using an adversarial learning-based segmentation model [22], and the segmentations were divided into patches of 2000 × 2000 pixels to create segmentation masks for all slides. From each whole-slide image, 25 non-overlapping patches, each measuring 2000 × 2000 pixels, are randomly selected to facilitate the extraction of imaging features. These patches and their corresponding segmentation masks were processed using the CellProﬁler software [6] to compute fea- tures related to cell and nucleus intensity, shape, and texture. Examples of extracted features include area, eccentricity, compactness, Euler number, and Zernike moments. Stain- speciﬁc intensity features were separately calculated for hematoxylin (H) and eosin (E). A 5-bin histogram summa- rization was performed for 215 extracted features, resulting in a 1075-dimensional feature vector per patient. Nuclei seg- mentation data was available for 978 patients, and 973 patient samples were successfully processed through CellProﬁler, forming the dataset used in this study without additional ﬁltering. RNA-seq data for all TCGA-BRCA patients was downloaded from the FireBrowse platform. Gene variabil- ity was determined by calculating the coefﬁcient of variation (σ/μ) for log2-transformed expression values. Genomic fea- ture vectors were constructed by selecting the top 500, 800, 1000, and 3000 most variable genes, and their z-scores were used for representation. Additionally, protein–protein inter- action data from the STRING database was incorporated to account for biological dependencies among genes, with inter- action weights providing insights into their relationships. For evaluation, we employed 5-fold cross-validation, dividing the dataset into training (544 patients), valida- tion (137 patients), and testing (292 patients) sets. We believe there are a few key fac- tors contributing to this outcome. Data Insufﬁciency: Despite using over 900 patient samples from TCGA-BRCA, the sample size remains relatively small for training deep and highly parameterized models like transformers or attention- based architectures. These models typically require much larger datasets to generalize effectively. Overﬁtting: The high capacity of these models likely led to overﬁtting on train- ing data. While we applied basic regularization (dropout and weight decay), more advanced strategies such as early stopping, stronger regularizers, or data augmentation for genomic and imaging features were not fully explored due to resource constraints. We will consider these in future itera- tions. Lack of Pretraining: We did not use pretrained weights for the transformer components or attention modules. Given the limited data, initializing these models from pretrained representations, especially on related tasks, could signif- icantly improve performance. We acknowledge important limitations in our statistical evaluation approach. While we employed 5-fold cross-validation and reported mean perfor- 123 30 Page 16 of 20 Complex & Intelligent Systems (2026) 12 :30 mance metrics, we did not perform formal statistical testing (ANOVA or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests) to compare perfor- mance differences between fusion methods. Without such paired statistical tests, we cannot deﬁnitively claim statistical signiﬁcance of performance differences between methods. However, the consistent ranking of methods across mul- tiple evaluation metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) and the statistically signiﬁcant survival stratiﬁcation provide reason- able conﬁdence in our comparative conclusions. Future work will implement paired statistical testing by storing individual fold results to enable rigorous inter-method comparisons. Implications and future directions The results highlight the effectiveness of multi-modal fusion in predicting breast cancer survival, with late fusion showing signiﬁcant promise. Expand- ing dataset size and diversity may also improve model applicability in clinical settings. Future work could incor- porate additional data types, such as clinical information (e.g., hormone receptor status, treatment details), radiomics, or longitudinal data, to enhance generalizability. While this study treats survival prediction as a binary classiﬁcation task, future efforts might adopt time-to-event frameworks like DeepSurv or CoxPH-based neural networks for contin- uous risk assessment. Additionally, pretraining large models on related histopathology datasets and leveraging domain- adaptive transformers could further enhance performance on smaller biomedical datasets. Discussion and conclusions In this study, we applied multi-modal approaches across early, intermediate, late, and hybrid fusion strategies to pre- dict breast cancer survival using combined histopathology and genomic features. The results, presented in Tables 3, 4, and 5, show that late fusion methods consistently achieve higher performance across all major evaluation metrics. Among them, the meta-learning-based late fusion approach yields the best overall results, with the highest accuracy (64.38%), F1-score (68.10%), and AUC (64.47%). Addi- tionally, survival stratiﬁcation using Kaplan–Meier analysis and log-rank tests (Table 6) demonstrates that this method achieves statistically signiﬁcant separation of high- and low-risk groups, further reinforcing its effectiveness. We introduced a hybrid fusion method that combines fusion methods into a uniﬁed architecture. This combination strat- egy processes histopathology data via a CNN, genomic data via an MLP, incorporates a cross-attention mechanism to capture inter-modal relationships, and concludes with a meta-learning classiﬁer. This hybrid approach, illustrated in Fig.3 and detailed in section “Combination strategy: a hybrid fusion approach”. We implemented a comprehensive eval- uation framework to compare these fusion methods using standard classiﬁcation metrics and survival analysis. While the comparisons between individual methods are detailed within each fusion category, we also provide a visual sum- mary in Fig.12 that presents the best-performing method from each category side by side. This comparison, com- bined with the full set of classiﬁcation metrics and survival analyses, offers a comprehensive and interpretable basis for concluding that the late fusion strategy is the most robust and reliable in this context. To enhance interpretabil- ity, we performed a SHAP feature

While the comparisons between individual methods are detailed within each fusion category, we also provide a visual sum- mary in Fig.12 that presents the best-performing method from each category side by side. This comparison, com- bined with the full set of classiﬁcation metrics and survival analyses, offers a comprehensive and interpretable basis for concluding that the late fusion strategy is the most robust and reliable in this context. To enhance interpretabil- ity, we performed a SHAP feature importance analysis on the hybrid model. This analysis revealed that genomic fea- tures contribute approximately 72–73% and imaging features 28–29% to the model’s predictions, with both modalities offering complementary insights. Figures10 and 11 visualize the global and individual feature importances, respectively, providing transparency into the model’s decision-making process and aiding clinical interpretability. Kaplan–Meier survival analysis revealed statistically sig- niﬁcant differences in survival distributions for most fusion methods, with notable exceptions such as Hybrid CNN–MLP Models and Multi-Modal Transformer Networks, which lacked statistical signiﬁcance. We acknowledge that multi- ple log-rank tests were performed across different methods. Although we did not apply formal correction methods (e.g., Bonferroni [16] or False Discovery Rate (FDR) [4]), most of the signiﬁcant results have p-values well below the con- ventional threshold (e.g., p < 0.01), suggesting that the conclusions remain robust. This limitation is noted, and future work may incorporate correction strategies to further strengthen statistical inference. As a classical baseline, we compared our methods with the sparse canonical correla- tion analysis (CCA)-based fusion method by Subramanian et al. [36], which represents a non-deep-learning approach to multi-modal survival prediction. The results, included in Table 4, show that other intermediate fusion models outper- form this classical fusion baseline, highlighting the beneﬁt of modality-aware representation learning in this context. The relatively lower performance of more complex models, such as the Multi-Modal Transformer and Cross-Attention-based networks, may be attributed to overﬁtting due to limited train- ing data. These models typically require larger datasets and beneﬁt from pretraining and advanced regularization, which were not applied in this study. One limitation of our study is the absence of other datasets. While METABRIC [13] provides genomic and clinical data, it lacks matched histopathology images, which restricts its suitability for validating our fusion models. Future work will focus on identifying or assembling datasets that include both modalities to enable external validation of multi-modal approaches and assess generalizability. Overall, this study presents a comprehensive analysis of multi-modal fusion strategies for breast cancer survival pre- diction,leveragingearly,intermediate,late,andhybridfusion approaches. Our results demonstrate that the choice of fusion strategy should be informed by task complexity, modality interaction, and data characteristics. The hybrid combination method introduced here offers a ﬂexible and interpretable framework that integrates complementary fusion principles, achieving both high predictive accuracy and robust sur- vival stratiﬁcation. All code and model conﬁgurations are publicly available, supporting reproducibility and further research. Future directions include integrating additional data types (e.g., clinical variables, radiomics), reﬁning model interpretability, and advancing toward clinical translation in precision oncology. method_struct.paper_summary: This study evaluates deep learning based fusion strategies, early, intermediate, late, and a hybrid approach, to integrate histopathology images and genomic data for one year survival prediction. The study develops a robust evaluation framework, employing tailored deep learning architectures and metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC.
